bins.name: No cost-shifting to ratepayers
bins.desc: LPPC stresses that its members use tariffs, special contracts, customer-funded infrastructure, take-or-pay provisions, minimum billing demand charges, and exit fees to ensure large loads pay their fair share and do not burden existing ratepayers. It presents these as proven mechanisms already protecting everyday customers.
quotes: its member utilities have long employed public power tariffs, special contracts, and bilateral service agreements to integrate large loads while protecting all customers from undue cost-
a summary of the tariff and contractual frameworks those utilities are using to ensure large load customers pay their fair share

LPPC member utilities are employing a variety of customer-protective mechanisms, including dedicated substations, upfront infrastructure funding requirements, take-or-pay provisions, minimum billing demand charges, exit fees, and bring-your-own-power arrangements, to ensure large load growth does not burden existing ratepayers.
Requires the large load customer to pay upfront for dedicated substations, distribution upgrades, transmission lines, and other required facilities.
Bills the customer at a minimum demand level (e.g., fixed percentage of expected peak demand), even if actual usage falls short.

bins.name: Protecting existing ratepayers
bins.desc: The filing frames its customer-protective mechanisms as the way to ensure large load growth does not shift costs onto other customers. It catalogs dedicated substations, upfront funding, and other provisions as in active use to protect all customers.
quotes: its member utilities have long employed public power tariffs, special contracts, and bilateral service agreements to integrate large loads while protecting all customers from undue cost-
a summary of the tariff and contractual frameworks those utilities are using to ensure large load customers pay their fair share

LPPC member utilities are employing a variety of customer-protective mechanisms, including dedicated substations, upfront infrastructure funding requirements, take-or-pay provisions, minimum billing demand charges, exit fees, and bring-your-own-power arrangements, to ensure large load growth does not burden existing ratepayers.

bins.name: Financial assurance and deposits
bins.desc: LPPC describes financial assurance practices, including required collateral or creditworthiness demonstrations before construction and upfront funding commitments, as standard tools its members use to ensure large loads can cover their costs.
quotes: LPPC member utilities are employing a variety of customer-protective mechanisms, including dedicated substations, upfront infrastructure funding requirements, take-or-pay provisions, minimum billing demand charges, exit fees, and bring-your-own-power arrangements, to ensure large load growth does not burden existing ratepayers.
Requires the customer to post collateral or demonstrate creditworthiness prior to construction.

bins.name: Defer to state and utility frameworks
bins.desc: LPPC argues states and utilities have already developed effective large load frameworks before any federal mandate and that federal policy should build on rather than disrupt this progress. It positions existing state and local processes as the foundation any federal action should respect.
quotes: Both approved tariffs and contract-based structures are in active use across multiple states, demonstrating that states and utilities have developed effective frameworks before any federal mandate.
We continue to underscore the importance of crafting federal policies that build upon, rather than disrupt, this progress.

bins.name: Existing frameworks already work
bins.desc: The comment supplements the record with evidence that approved tariffs and contract structures are in active use across multiple states, presenting them as proof the issue is already being managed without federal intervention.
quotes: Both approved tariffs and contract-based structures are in active use across multiple states, demonstrating that states and utilities have developed effective frameworks before any federal mandate.
We continue to underscore the importance of crafting federal policies that build upon, rather than disrupt, this progress.

bins.name: Bring-your-own-power requirements
bins.desc: LPPC highlights bring-your-own-power arrangements that require large load customers to procure or contract for their own generation, insulating other customers from capacity cost impacts.
quotes: Requires large load customers to procure or contract for their own generation supply.

bins.name: Customer-procured generation
bins.desc: The filing describes generation requirements and customer-owned supply arrangements among the protective tools members use to serve large loads.
quotes: Requires large load customers to procure or contract for their own generation supply.

bins.name: MW eligibility thresholds vary
bins.desc: LPPC notes that member tariffs define qualifying large load customers by minimum power demand thresholds ranging from 5 MW to 75 or more MW, supporting the use of size-based eligibility to apply special terms only to the largest customers.
quotes: Defines the type of large load customer that qualifies, such as a minimum power demand (ranging from 5 MW to 75+ MW across LPPC members).

bins.name: Customer-funded grid upgrades
bins.desc: The comment describes requirements that large load customers pay upfront for dedicated substations, distribution upgrades, and transmission lines so the upgrades needed to serve them do not fall on existing ratepayers.
quotes: Requires the large load customer to pay upfront for dedicated substations, distribution upgrades, transmission lines, and other required facilities.

bins.name: Faster, fairer interconnection
bins.desc: LPPC supports improving the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections and aligns itself with the goal of integrating new demand responsibly.
quotes: We share the Administration's commitment to energy dominance and specifically support improving the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections.

bins.name: Speed and predictability
bins.desc: The filing supports faster and more predictable large load interconnection as a shared goal, while emphasizing it should be achieved through existing customer-protective frameworks.
quotes: We share the Administration's commitment to energy dominance and specifically support improving the speed, efficiency, fairness, and predictability of large load interconnections.
